articles.title: Coordination Without Convergence
articles.description: The central idea of the Unity Compass investigation. Coordination does not require value convergence as a prerequisite.

articles.title: Logos
articles.description: Ancient Greek concept of cosmic ordering principle. The investigation argues that the progressive narrowing of logos to formal logic is upstream of coordination theory's incompleteness.

articles.title: Path Dependence
articles.description: The structural barrier whereby institutions resist change because the longer they operate under given assumptions, the more costly it becomes to switch. Identified in the investigation as one of five compound barriers to coordination reform.

articles.title: Restorative Justice
articles.description: Coordination between people who share nothing but consequences. Demonstrates that coordination across the most extreme difference is possible when the process is structured to make room for what formal theory excludes.

articles.title: Structural Interdependence
articles.description: The investigation's central hypothesis. Structural features of interdependence as candidate grounds for coordination without value convergence.

articles.title: Value Incommensurability
articles.description: The condition where agents hold values that cannot be ranked on a common scale. The philosophical problem the investigation's central question addresses.

articles.title: Decision Theory Under Severe Uncertainty
articles.description: Formal frameworks for rational decision-making when standard expected utility breaks down. Central to the investigation's sub-question on rationality.

articles.title: Evolutionary Game Theory
articles.description: The study of how cooperative norms emerge and stabilise in populations of interacting agents. Central to the investigation's sub-question on stability.

articles.title: Game Theory
articles.description: The dominant formal framework for analysing strategic interaction. The investigation builds on its vocabulary while identifying four foundational assumptions that break under value incommensurability.

articles.title: Mechanism Design
articles.description: Sometimes called "reverse game theory." Designs rules of interaction to achieve desired outcomes given strategic actors. Breaks when objectives are contested or values are incommensurable.

articles.title: Social Choice Theory
articles.description: The mathematical study of how individual preferences can be aggregated into collective decisions. Its impossibility results set formal limits on what coordination mechanisms can achieve.

articles.title: Elinor Ostrom
articles.description: Nobel Prize-winning political economist. Her work on commons governance provides the most empirically grounded coordination research in existence.

articles.title: Heraclitus
articles.description: Pre-Socratic philosopher whose concept of logos as unity-in-opposition is the philosophical anchor for the investigation's critique of narrowed reason.

articles.title: J. McKenzie Alexander
articles.description: Professor of Philosophy at LSE. Target supervisor for the doctoral research. Specialist in evolutionary game theory and evolution of morality.

articles.title: Mancur Olson
articles.description: Political economist who demonstrated that shared interests do not reliably produce collective action. His work grounds the investigation's claim that value convergence is not sufficient for coordination.

articles.title: Michael Bratman
articles.description: Philosopher whose shared cooperative activity theory is the primary alternative account of what grounds coordination. The investigation argues structural interdependence can explain why Bratman's conditions succeed when they do.

articles.title: Richard Bradley
articles.description: Professor of Philosophy at LSE. Target supervisor for the doctoral research. Specialist in decision theory, formal epistemology and social choice.

articles.title: Robert Axelrod
articles.description: Political scientist whose iterated prisoner's dilemma tournaments demonstrated that cooperation can emerge without central authority. His four conditions for cooperation are a key reference point the investigation builds on and identifies limitations of.

articles.title: Ruth Chang
articles.description: Philosopher of practical reason. Her work on hard choices and parity provides the philosophical framework for value incommensurability the investigation builds on.

articles.title: Thomas Schelling
articles.description: Economist and Nobel laureate whose work on focal points demonstrated that coordination can emerge through structural salience without shared values or communication.

articles.title: AUKUS
articles.description: Trilateral security partnership between Australia, UK and USA. The investigation's primary case study for testing coordination without convergence.

articles.title: Fukushima Daiichi
articles.description: The 2011 nuclear disaster as a case study in compounding coordination failure modes. Information distortion, regulatory capture, path dependence and the suppression of situated judgment.

articles.title: Irish Citizens' Assembly
articles.description: Ireland's randomly-selected citizens' assemblies produced constitutional recommendations on deeply divisive issues that elected politicians had avoided for decades.

articles.title: Montreal Protocol
articles.description: The 1987 international agreement to phase out ozone-depleting substances. Widely regarded as the most successful international environmental agreement and the investigation's strongest evidence for planetary-scale coordination without convergence.

articles.title: IETF and Rough Consensus
articles.description: The Internet Engineering Task Force coordinates thousands of engineers on internet standards using "rough consensus and running code." A mechanism that acknowledges objections without requiring resolution.
